author: luca_zanolini
content:
> In principle this is not a big issue

Why don't you think it's too serious? The IGNORE rule only prevents the duplicate envelope, not the actual attack, or an I missing something? the 49/51 split still goes through and breaks canonicity of the next slot. Unless we're assuming all broadcasts propagate uniformly?

author: luca_zanolini
content:
Would this issue be solved by requiring the PTC to recompute the execution block hash before voting `payload_present=True`, without fully executing the payload?

My understanding is that the execution `block_hash` is computed as `keccak256(rlp(header))`, where the header commits to fields such as `transactions_root` etc . So the PTC would not need to execute the transactions; it would only need to reconstruct the header commitments from the received payload/envelope and check:

computed_block_hash == payload.block_hash == bid.block_hash

If that check passed before voting, then a payload whose declared `payload.block_hash` matches the bid but whose actual contents hash to something else would not receive a positive timeliness vote.

Am I missing any reason why this check is too expensive or insufficient for the PTC deadline?

author: tersec
content: Generically, this would be the first time the consensus specs required RLP hashing of this sort

author: tersec
content: Whether one things this is a blocking issue is subjective, but it's certainly not a minor one

author: luca_zanolini
content: I see. Yeah I was just wondering because if this attack can be placed ‘easily’, then it’s quite bad. For instance without additional assumptions on the honesty of the previous slot builder, some properties would break. That’s why I was asking why it’s not a big issue

author: potuz
content: Doing the rlp hash will still be irrelevant probably. The builder can invalidate other parts of the timely Payload

author: potuz
content: Ah nvm everything else may be covered by the hash

author: luca_zanolini

author: nflaig
content: can't we simply add a gossip check for this, that does the rlp validation?

author: tersec
content: "simply"

author: tersec
content: For the record, Nimbus does check RLP in opt sync scenarios. But it's not a simple addition

author: potuz
content:
what proofs? you mean your document? yeah definitely they must be wrong if they do not account for payload equivocation. The reason I believe that the builder can't really trigger this off so easily is that they need to maintain a tight control over the gossip network:
1) They need exactly 49% of the PTC to get the invalid payload first.
2) They need exactly 51% of the attesters to get the valid payload first. 
3) They need to get the proposer to be in the first set. 
4) They need to do this with the payloads being quite separated in time to trigger this. 
I believe the combination of these events is impossible to trigger

author: tersec
content: and Ethereum keeps almost-breaking the ability to do so by not providing the necessary information from the EL side blocks

author: tersec
content: to properly replicate the EL blockhashes

author: tersec
content: so if people actually want this isn't in no way "simple"

author: tersec
content: and it requires cross-layer coordination

author: potuz
content: So I think you guys may be exaggerating, but yes, it's worth analyzing what can come out of this, I believe ex-antee reorgs are far fetched, but perhaps some other form of attack that I can't see right now

content: which is why I pinged Francesco

author: luca_zanolini
content:
Yes, the proofs do not yet account for a few aspects and are still quite sketchy at this stage. That said, I also believe that some effects of builder equivocation may be mitigated by the underlying network assumptions. For example, is it reasonable to assume that broadcasts (all broadcasts) propagate sufficiently uniformly across the network? Or it isn’t a fair assumption?

In any case, I agree that this attack requires substantial power on the builder’s side. Nevertheless, if it does not violate any of the assumptions, then it represents a valid attack vector and should be addressed.

author: luca_zanolini
content: I’m not really the best person here for advocating for something like this 🙉 so I trust your judgment.. I wanted first to think of a theoretical solution
